definition: Carry and momentum FX factors exhibit conditional correlation that flips from -0.3 in normal conditions (natural hedge) to +0.6 during crises (simultaneous reversal). Multi-factor FX diversification is a fair-weather benefit that disappears precisely during the sudden-stop episodes that determine long-run wealth outcomes.
evidence: Normal conditions: momentum naturally hedges carry as signals reverse during depreciation
Analogous to equity-bond correlation breakdown in risk-off regimes
0.90 swing magnitude described as largest documented factor correlation regime change in any asset class
Portfolio construction implication: explicit tail hedging required as third factor allocation
open_questions: Can alternative factor construction preserve the diversification benefit in stress regimes?
What is the transition speed from negative to positive correlation — is there a usable warning signal?
Whether explicit tail hedging is cost-effective as replacement for lost fair-weather diversification
